Here's an example from the business world of how public commitments amplify the principle of consistency. It involves a Fortune 500 company with a problem. Its sales team was significantly off their sales targets for the month of February, performing at only 70 percent of the desired target. For help in the future, they called in Dr. Niter, who arranged for a meeting of all the salespeople where the company's CEO asked each one to write down and then publicly announce a sales target for the following month that they felt comfortable with, saying, please stay out loud your personal target for next month. Any level is fine, we just want to know what you're aiming for. When the sum of the individual targets was totaled, it was even higher than the target the company had hoped for. But the real test came at the end of the next month when the sales figures came in. Not only did sales meet the new target, they actually exceeded it by a healthy amount. Now, although this example concerns sales targets, the power of publicly stated commitments can work for almost any goal you are truly willing to spend the effort to achieve. Why? Because it puts you on record in the eyes of the people whose positive regard you want. You want to show them that you are someone who lives up to your commitments, something we all want to do.
